card.intuition: Reduce model memory and speed up inference by representing weights/activations in low bit-width. Uniform quantization: q = round(x/scale + zero_point), where scale = (x_max−x_min)/(2^b−1). INT8 reduces memory by 4× vs FP32 with <1% accuracy loss. Post-training quantization (PTQ) requires no retraining; Quantization-aware training (QAT) simulates quantization during training for better accuracy.
card.analogy: Compressing a photograph by reducing colour depth: from 16 million colours (FP32) to 256 colours (INT8). The image looks nearly identical for most uses and loads 4× faster. QAT is like training a painter to work within the colour constraint from the start.
card.time_complexity: O(n) to quantize weights; O(n) inference with INT8 SIMD vs O(n) FP32
card.space_complexity: FP32: 4 bytes/param → INT8: 1 byte/param; INT4: 0.5 bytes/param
when_to_use: Edge deployment: mobile, embedded, IoT with limited memory
Inference acceleration: INT8 matrix multiply is 2-4× faster on modern hardware
LLM serving: 4-bit quantization (GPTQ, AWQ) enables 70B models on single GPU
gotchas: Outlier activations in LLMs break uniform quantization — use SmoothQuant to redistribute
INT4 per-group quantization: separate scale per group of 128 weights reduces error
QAT requires straight-through estimator (STE) to backpropagate through rounding

exercise.prompt: Implement symmetric INT8 quantization and dequantization.
exercise.starter_code:
import math

def quantize_int8(tensor, scale=None):
    """
    Symmetric INT8 quantization.
    scale = max(|tensor|) / 127
    Returns (quantized: list of ints, scale: float).
    """
    if scale is None:
        max_val = max(abs(x) for x in tensor)
        scale   = max_val / 127.0 if max_val > 0 else 1.0
    quantized = [max(-128, min(127, round(x / scale))) for x in tensor]
    return quantized, scale

def dequantize_int8(quantized, scale):
    """
    Reconstruct FP32 approximation from INT8 values.
    """
    return [___________  for q in quantized]
hints: Dequantization simply multiplies the integer value by the scale: x_approx = q · scale.
exercise.test_code:
import math

tensor = [0.5, -0.3, 1.2, -1.0, 0.0]
q, scale = quantize_int8(tensor)

# INT8 range
assert all(-128 <= v <= 127 for v in q)
# Scale = 1.2/127
assert abs(scale - 1.2/127) < 1e-6

# Dequantize
recon = dequantize_int8(q, scale)
for orig, r in zip(tensor, recon):
    assert abs(orig - r) < 0.02, f'Quantization error too large: {orig:.4f} vs {r:.4f}'

# Zero maps to zero
assert dequantize_int8([0], scale)[0] == 0.0
print('PASS')
